Category: Agriculture & Resources: Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing, Mining, Energy
Description: Risks of fake audio/video instructions to field workers or operators.
Prerequisites: Learners should have a basic familiarity with day-to-day operations in an agricultural, forestry, fishing, mining, or energy setting, along with the ability to use a smartphone or computer to access online training materials.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, learners will be able to identify the key characteristics of deepfake audio and video content and explain how fraudulent instructions can pose serious safety and operational risks to field workers in agriculture, forestry, fishing, mining, and energy sectors. Learners will also apply practical verification strategies to assess the authenticity of instructions received through digital channels, helping to protect themselves and their colleagues from harm in high-risk working environments.

lessons.Description: This lesson introduces the growing threat of deepfake audio and video content in operational and field-based working environments. You will explore how convincingly fabricated instructions, delivered through voice messages, video calls, or recorded briefings, can deceive workers into taking unsafe or unauthorised actions. The lesson covers real-world scenarios where deepfakes have been used to impersonate managers, supervisors, or clients, and explains why these attacks are particularly dangerous in time-pressured or remote settings. By the end of this lesson, you will be able to recognise the warning signs of fake instructions and understand the practical steps you can take to verify the source before acting. No prior technical knowledge is required, and all concepts are explained in plain, straightforward language.
lessons.content: Risks of fake audio/video instructions to field workers or operators.

slides.subtitle: When Seeing Is No Longer Believing
slides.slidecontent:
• Realistic fake videos and audio clips are now easy to create and hard to spot
• Field workers and operators increasingly receive instructions via digital channels
• A convincing fake voice or face can trigger dangerous real-world actions
• The threat is real
• This course explains what deepfakes are, how they appear in operational settings, and what you can do about them

slides.subtitle: What to Watch For in Video Instructions
slides.slidecontent:
• Lip sync looks slightly off or delayed
• Facial edges flicker or appear blurry
• Unnatural blinking patterns (too frequent, rare, or oddly timed)
• Lighting shifts inconsistently mid-sentence
• Background details blur or glitch unexpectedly

slides.subtitle: What to Listen For in Voice Instructions
slides.slidecontent:
• Voice sounds flat, robotic, or unnaturally smooth
• Background noise cuts abruptly or sounds too clean
• Voice pitch feels "off" or inconsistent
• Words seem clipped or artificially stitched together
• Caller rushes you or creates unusual pressure

slides.subtitle: When Instructions Don't Feel Right
slides.slidecontent:
• Request feels out of character for that person
• Unusual urgency without clear justification
• Bypassing normal approval processes
• Instructions contradict established safety protocols
• Caller avoids answering direct questions about details

slides.subtitle: How Deepfakes Target Field Operations
slides.slidecontent:
• Fake supervisor voice messages about equipment changes
• Video calls impersonating managers during shift changes
• False emergency instructions from supposed safety officers
• Fraudulent client requests for operational modifications
• Counterfeit training videos with dangerous procedures

slides.subtitle: Double-Check Before You Act
slides.slidecontent:
• Call the person back using a known, trusted number
• Verify through official company communication channels
• Check with a second supervisor or colleague
• Ask specific questions only the real person would know
• Follow standard authorization procedures regardless of urgency

slides.subtitle: Speak Up, Stay Safe, Act Fast
slides.slidecontent:
• Report to your line manager or designated security contact immediately
• Use your organisation's official incident reporting channel
• Never delete the suspicious message before logging it
• Screenshot or record source details if safe to do so
• Speed matters - early reports help protect the whole team

slides.subtitle: Essential Habits for Staying Protected
slides.slidecontent:
• Always verify instructions through a second, trusted channel before acting
• Pause if something feels off - trust your instincts
• Unusual urgency is a common tactic to bypass judgment
• Check the source, not just the message content
• Report suspected deepfakes immediately, even if unsure
• Staying alert protects you and your whole team

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on deepfakes in operational contexts. Technology has advanced to the point where realistic fake videos and audio recordings can be created quickly, cheaply, and convincingly. For people working in the field or in operational roles, this matters enormously, because more and more of us receive instructions through digital channels, whether that is a voice message, a video call, or a short clip sent to a device. If someone can fake a manager's voice or a supervisor's face well enough to fool you, they could potentially trigger a real and dangerous response on the ground. This is not a distant or theoretical problem; it is happening now. Throughout this course, we will look at what deepfakes actually are, how they show up in workplace settings, and most importantly, what you can do to protect yourself and your colleagues.

Let's start with video deepfakes and what to watch for. One of the most reliable indicators is the mouth - if the lip movements look slightly out of step with the words being spoken, that's a strong warning signal. Pay attention to the edges of the person's face as well. Deepfakes frequently struggle with fine detail, so you might notice the outline of someone's face flickering as they move or appearing blurry around the hairline or jawline. Watch the eyes too, because unnatural blinking patterns are something deepfake technology still struggles to get right. Finally, observe the lighting. In a genuine recording, light behaves consistently, but in a deepfake you may see it shift or change in ways that simply don't make sense.

Now let's focus on audio deepfakes and what to listen for. Deepfake audio often sounds slightly robotic or unnaturally flat, lacking the natural rhythm and warmth of a real person speaking. You might also notice the background is strangely silent or too clean, because synthetic audio often strips out ambient noise that would normally be there. Pay attention to how words flow together - you might catch words that seem clipped or cut off abruptly, which can happen when audio segments are artificially stitched together. And if the voice pitch feels slightly wrong about how the voice sounds, trust that instinct. These audio clues can be just as telling as visual ones.

Beyond technical indicators, pay attention to behavioral red flags that suggest something isn't right. Does the request actually sound like something that person would normally ask? If it feels out of character, that's a serious warning sign. Are they rushing you, pushing you to act quickly without giving you time to think, or trying to bypass normal procedures? Genuine supervisors and managers typically follow established protocols, especially for safety-critical instructions. If someone is avoiding your direct questions about details or creating unusual pressure to act immediately, these are behavioral patterns worth noting and acting on.

Let me give you some real-world examples of how deepfakes are being used to target field operations. Attackers might create fake voice messages that appear to come from your supervisor, giving new instructions about equipment usage or safety procedures. You might receive video calls from someone who looks like your manager during shift changes, asking you to modify operations in ways that seem urgent. There have been cases of fake emergency instructions supposedly from safety officers, and fraudulent client requests that ask workers to change established procedures. Even training materials aren't immune - counterfeit training videos with dangerous procedures have been used to mislead workers.

So how do you verify if an instruction is genuine? The gold standard is to call the person back using a number you know and trust, not the one they called from. You can also verify through your organization's official communication channels - check the company directory or internal messaging system. When in doubt, ask a second supervisor or colleague to confirm the instruction. Don't be afraid to ask specific questions that only the real person would know the answer to. And regardless of how urgent something seems, follow your standard authorization procedures. Legitimate emergencies will still allow time for proper verification.

If you suspect you've received a deepfake instruction, act quickly but systematically. Go straight to your line manager or your organization's designated security contact - don't just mention it to a colleague in passing. Use the official incident reporting channel your organization has in place, because that creates a proper record. Here's something crucial: do not delete or dismiss that suspicious message. Leave it exactly as it is for investigation. If you can safely take a screenshot or note down the source details, do that too. Speed genuinely matters because the sooner a suspicious instruction is reported, the sooner your security team can step in and protect everyone.

Let's wrap up with the essential habits that will keep you protected. The most important practice is to always verify instructions through a second, trusted channel before acting on them. If something feels off, even slightly, pause and trust that instinct. Remember that attackers often use urgency and pressure to rush you into making decisions before you have time to think clearly. Don't just examine the message content - always check the source as well. If you ever suspect something might be a deepfake attempt, report it immediately. You don't need to be certain - even a suspicion is worth raising. Staying alert protects not just yourself, but everyone around you too.

Who is this for: Field workers, equipment operators, site supervisors, agricultural workers, forestry technicians, mining operators, energy plant operators, facility managers, shift supervisors
Team or Dept this is for: Operations, Field Operations, Production, Site Management, Health and Safety, Agriculture, Mining, Energy Operations, Facilities Management
